sentences.text: That's right.
He said-- I was surprised because I had not given him the number of the apartment, [BACKGROUND NOISE] only the number of the house.
It was a huge house, um, without a family name.
In the first place, I wanted to mention that he asked my father-- he saw my father and he said, do Jewish people also live here?
Because my father looked so non-Jewish that he thought it's a Christian.
My father said, we are Jews.
So is Zelda living here?
Yes.
Your daughter?
Yes.
So he said, I have come to ask you if you need help.
And I was very angry and very proud.
I said, thank you very much.
I don't need any help.
We have got everything.
And then what he said was so surprising.
He said the old city of Riga has been bombed and I have lost my furnished room.
Could you perhaps help me finding a room among your acquaintances?
So I thought, that's a miracle.

All Latvian officers go into any Jewish apartment, rob the apartment, kill the people, and here is someone standing in front of me, asking me, the poor Jewish girl, to get a room for him, so it means something.
And I said, oh, I think I may help, be able to help you.
And I rang at Mrs.
Joffer's door.
Her husband had been killed the first night and she was-- she survived her husband with her four children.
And she had a very large apartment and she was happy when I suggested she take this man.
And so he moved in.
But we were almost-- we weren't in touch because I left in the morning, came home in the evening.
He never dropped in to ask questions.
Sometimes I went in to see Mrs.
Joffer.
Sometimes I stayed in her husband's bed.
She asked me to stay overnight because she was scared.

And he wrote down, on the door, Ianis Alexander Vaboulis [NON-ENGLISH], which meant she was defended, defended-- that a Latvian lived there, so Latvian militia, policeman wouldn't be able to think of coming and robbing her apartment or beating her up.
And she was happy with that.
He worked in buildings, a building office.
Got very little money.
And my nanny knew him already.
She met him when she was in our house.
Now and again, she sent some food from the countryside, where she was working, to his place.
I went-- the German officer who wanted to kill me was right.
Sometimes in the dark, I took off the yellow patch, which was not far away, where he lived, I went to his place.
I got a potato or something like that and I got it to the ghetto.
So in this way, he helped me, that he received a parcel and he waited for me to come and pick it up.
Oh, that was very early.
That was in August.
Yes.
And he remained there.
And then, it so happened that since the apartment is very large and there are not sufficient apartments in Riga for the occupying forces, he had to look for somebody to share the apartment with.
And here comes the story of Edgars Kraujinsh and Laimons Ledums.
Edgars Kraujinsh was his school friend and Laimons-- and he knew Laimons Ledums as well.
He had met them and he shared the apartment without knowing where they were working and what they were doing.
No.
He was staying with Mrs.
Joffer-- Mrs.
Joffer was staying with him until she moved to the ghetto.
After she had moved to the ghetto, he was all alone in that apartment and that was too much for a man, for one single person.
So he-- occasionally, he met these two friends who needed an apartment and this is why he shared apartment with people he knew.
And later, he found out what these people were doing.
No, no, I only felt that something is going to happen, but not an actor, not no way.
Only when we returned, they were standing two lines of Latvian Schutzsorte, these policeman and they said, gave the command, run!
And we were all running and they had rubber clubs in their hands and they were hitting us-- not me.
And now, again, a question.
It was dark.
Suppose they liked my face and they were sorry for me.
They didn't see my face.
I will never forget it.
The moment where the man raised the rubber club in order to beat me.
His hand went down like this and I was the only one among the people who was not beaten.
This makes no difference, but that's interesting.
I ran for my life.
Then I come home and my father is standing and waiting for me and next morning, I say goodbye, knowing it's the last time I see my parents and my sister.
And then I come to the Germans' place, between the two double doors, and I'm going to Vaboulis.
And Vaboulis says no, you are not going anywhere.
Your nanny and I are going to-- we are going to hide you.
And immediately, he contacted my nanny.
He knew where she lived in the countryside and she came immediately to Stabu iela Six and there we were staying together.
In the first place, we were afraid to stay there for a long time because they were the same neighbors we had had before the war, who knew me.
I didn't go out, but we were afraid.
Perhaps through the window, perhaps, someone is coming and knocking the door and sees me somehow.
So we decided to move.
We means him, not me.
Together, with two of these Latvians, moving to Miera iela 29.
Yes.
Please, with pleasure.
Edgars Kraujinsh, E-D-G-A-R-S Kraujinsh, K-R-A-U-J-I-N-S-H.
The next line is Laimons Lidums, L-A-I-M-O-N-S, Lidums, L-I-D-U-M-S.
All of us together.
But at the beginning, I am hidden in a separate room, a room without a handle.
But Vaboulis had the handle of the door.
At the beginning, they did not know who I was.
They knew that I was Vaboulis' fiance who does not work and who he is in love with.
And it never occurred to them that I am Jewish.
So they all, um, arranged parties almost every evening.
And there was a lot of vodka and everything and prostitutes and women and in general, it was a terrible way of life.
I was hiding in that room, but I heard every word that was said in the next room.
Ieva was in a small room in the house, but not constantly.
She had to go back to the countryside, again, to get some food and to arrange things and then she came back.
Now and again, so from time to time.
So Vaboulis went to work.
He was responsible for my life.
He knew that in case I was found, it is his death and his family's death.
And his family was in the countryside.
So I don't remember when it was, but he was obliged to say to these men, to tell them the truth, that I was Jewish.
And he was not afraid to do so.
For one, he knew that they were killing Jews in the ghetto.
He understood that they could not inform on me.
If they had informed on me, the police, the Germans would have said, so, where have you been so far?
If you knew, so you are living, sharing an apartment with a woman and you don't know who she is?
You don't know she's Jew?
Don't tell me lies.
And they-- their life was also endangered.
So this is why Vaboulis was not afraid of them.
There was no risk anymore.
There were evenings when Vaboulis was out and one of them was in.
So they used to sit and talk to me.
Yeah, very friendly.

[BACKGROUND NOISE] And told-- after having had vodka, always vodka-- they told me the truth, that they had been-- first of all, from them, I found out when [INAUDIBLE], the ghetto, was a second one when my family was killed.
It was the 8th of December, about the difference of more than a week.
They told me terrible stories, unbelievable to a normal human being.
For instance, Kraujinsh told me that he was holding-- a women went to death with her baby.
He was in a uniform, Kraujinsh, and the woman-- the woman couldn't go on with a baby.
It was a long way to walk, you know, to the ditch.
And the baby was crying, so he took the baby out of her arms to keep it in his arms.
He said it was such a nice baby.
And the baby was counting the buttons of his greatcoat.
And he said, in order to make an end to it, I killed the baby myself.
Such stories.
And little by little, I heard the names of their friends, of their collaborators.
They used to come to the house.
Particularly to Miera iela already, not so much to Stabu iela.
And they, those people had no idea who I was and they kept sitting and boasting how many people they've shot.
What a day.
And I knew exactly where they came from, what their names were, et cetera.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Oh, yes.
They were-- for instance, there was a name of Edgars Rijkurs.
You want me to spell the name?
E-D-G-A-R-S Rijkurs, R-I-J-K-U-R-S.
In the first place, very intelligent man, strange as it may seem, but many killers were intelligent, highly educated people.
In the beginning, he was just a Jew killer and then when things were-- got worse at the front, he went to the front to fight against the Russians, where he died.
Um, I know [INAUDIBLE].
I know a certain [INAUDIBLE].
I don't remember his first name.
I've got it somewhere.
These were all friends, Vaboulis' school friends-- either school friends or friends from the military academy.
No, they wanted to mobilize him.
He did.
So and this is what he was afraid of in the first place.
He did not want to fight with the Germans against the Russians.
He hated the Germans.
In the second place, he didn't know what to do with me.
So when he was drafted-- and he was a lieutenant.
He has never been in the army, never fought in any army, just finished-- graduated from military academy.
When he was drafted, the two of us went on a motorcycle to Madona-- that is about his birthplace.
M-A-D-O-N-A, Madona.
And there, he drank a lot of coffee, I remember.
And he was given, by a friend, a doctor, different tablets which made his heart beat and just before going in.
And then the man who was in charge said, what is your rank?
He said, I'm a corporal.
He was a lieutenant.
He said, anyone can come to this place and boast he's a corporal.
Prove you are a corporal.
Well, anyway, they found he was ill and he was absolutely healthy.
He was released home.
That was in 1942, '43.
So when he went on working, there might have only three Latvian officers I know of-- perhaps, there are some more.
That is Ianis Vaboulis, that is Ianis Selminsch, and that is [INAUDIBLE], who have not killed Jewish people.
The rest of them, all of the gang took part in killing Jews.
To go back to the place you lived with him, what was your timetable?
What were you doing the whole day?
That I can tell you easily.
First of all, it was a beautiful, small apartment.
You mean in Miera iela already?
So one very large room was shared by Kraujinsh and Ledums.
And two rooms belonged to us.
Actually, we lived only in one room because it was very cozy and very beautiful.
We were all constantly frightened.
We feared that my nanny, meaning very well, had liked to chat over a cup of tea and that she had told-- we counted about 16 or 17 people knew of my whereabouts.
So it was dangerous.
And we thought again of leaving the place, but where?
I usually-- he was at work.
I usually listened to the radio.
There was death sentence for listening to foreign radio, the BBC and even Moscow.
So I knew exactly what's going on in front.
I knew exactly what was happening where-- because I kept the radio near my ear and I knew it.
I learned, uh-- I learned French.
I studied for several years.
And I read a lot.
I did something in the household with my nanny and the days passed.
So and now comes the tragic day.
One day-- it was a Sunday in February 1943-- the doorbell rang.
And it was so organized in the house, as soon as the doorbell rang, I was tiptoeing to the [NON-ENGLISH], a small room near the kitchen.
My nanny was in the kitchen and she gave me a sign that SD, Sicherheitspolizei men, have come.
That is the Gestapo.
Luckily, the first things they did, they went to Laimons Lidums' room and Edgars Kraujinsh, who had lady friends there and they were checking their ID cards.
While they were checking their ID cards, the moment Vaboulis opened my door, he was absolutely-- his face was white.
He said, Zelda, this is the end.
I had no passport, nothing, no documents at all.
So what I did-- I was a genius, probably.
I took a kerchief, the kerchief of my nanny.
I put it on my head, like a girl from the countryside.
I held my feet like this, cross-legged.
I took the scissors that were on her bed and I was cutting my fingernails without looking at them.
And they opened the door, and 'Wer sind Sie?' Who are you?
I-- I thought-- my first thought was they should not know that I speak German, because Jewish people do speak German.
I said [SPEAKING GERMAN POORLY] He did this and he left me alone.
The moment he went back to the room, I opened the front door.
It was a very cold winter day in February in Riga.
I left the house.
I went along the street without a coat, without a hat, in a dress.
People-- Latvian people, Christian people, were coming from church.
Nobody paid any attention to me.
Nobody asked me who I was.
And I didn't know where to go.
And then it occurred to me that Mrs.
Steinbach, ?] a friend of mine, was hiding-- had been hiding in the place of a Dutchman, which was not too far away.
I knocked on his door.
He never seen me before and I said, the Gestapo is in my house.
They're going to arrest me if you don't let me in.
I'm a friend of Mrs.
Steinbach. ?] And he opened the door and he let me in-- a very cultured, very intelligent man.
And then he found Ianis for me in the evening and--
I believe hours, hours only.
The same day, same evening, he picked me up and we went back to that house.
Richard van Ham.
Of course.
R-I-C-H-A-R-D, now comes a small V-A-N capital H-A-M.
All right.
Yes, I did.
Yes, I did.

In the first place, I heard from Lidums and Kraujinsh that a street where my parents, where I had lived before, across a Jewish old cemetery, that all the inhabitants had already been killed in the Rumbula, that is on the 8th of December.
It means my family was not there.
And, uh, secondly-- and secondly, I was in touch with the ghetto.
So where I lived in Miera iela, my nanny was with us constantly.

She went down to get some potatoes, I believe, into the cellar and she saw Jewish people with the yellow patches working in the courtyard, because German officers lived upstairs and they were bringing wood for them and providing them with different things.
She, being a friend of the Jewish people, went up to the Jews and said, where are you from?
We are from the ghetto.
And she said, we are hiding a Jewish girl.
This was-- I think she shouldn't have said, but the purpose was to do something good.
So she wanted them to trust her that she wants to help them.
And she brought them to-- four or five people-- and she brought them upstairs and of course we fed.
And I asked one of them, his name was [NON-ENGLISH].
I asked him to find out who is still alive of our mutual acquaintances.
Who was the man you asked for?
[NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
[NON-ENGLISH], I told you who's on television, you know, that is his uncle.
He survived the war.
And the next day, he brought me a note written on sport cigarette paper, cigarette package.
Liebe Zelma, Dear Zelma, I'm happy you are in town.
Your parents and your sister, along with my parents and my sister [BACKGROUND NOISE] , were lining up for Rumbula on the night of the seventh through the eighth and they were all shot dead.
So I found-- and your mother, the last words I heard from her-- he was my boyfriend, so he knew my mother very well-- the last words I heard from your mother were, I'm happy Zelma is in town.
So this is how I found out the terrible way.
Pardon me?
Oh, Max Schwartz.
I have even his picture.
We had been friends for about three years, very close friends.
We were supposed to marry.
He survived the war until the last day, I believe.
So he was made by the Latvians or Germans to dig mass graves to burn the dead corpses.
And when the mass graves were dug, all the group was shot dead.
So he did not survive the last days of the war.
So in the first place, you should know that Latvians are drunkards.
There is no social gathering without a lot of vodka.
Actually, they're alcoholics and later on after the war, I found-- 1948, I found, in a ditch, very many empty bottles of vodka which were given to them probably by the Germans for shooting.
Almost each Latvian I know, almost, each Latvian is an alcoholic.
In the first place, they used to come together to eat, drink, and sing.
All their songs, even their national songs, not the anthem, but folk songs, had a parody against Jews, which throw the Jews into the river and let's kill them, something like that.
You can imagine what I felt like, sitting among them.
But I had only one aim and didn't think of anything that-- of anything they're saying.
Remember, remember forever their names, their surname, what schools they went to, what they were doing before the war, whatever you can.
You have a mission.
Why did the rest perish?
Why did you stay alive?
And your mission is the oath you have given at the mass graves in [NON-ENGLISH], that if you stay alive, you will destroy as many of them as you can.
And this is what I did.
There were no sexual subjects.
Now and again a stupid, absolutely stupid, naive anecdote, a story of the front line, a story of whom they had killed, how many people they had killed at a certain night.
Um, nothing about their families.
Stupid, limited, stupid conversation of very primitive people, though highly educated.
No.
No, many of them were married.
No.
No, no, no, no, no.
They were-- there was-- they were sleeping with occasional women.
Sometimes they brought the women, the women they were sleeping with, to Lidums' and Kraujinsh's bed.
And I, sitting in my room, I knew about it.
It was a terrible feeling I had, as if I were in a whorehouse, you know, sometimes, being obliged to stay there in order to save my life.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
They trusted me a lot these, two men.
They thought later on that if the Germans lose the war-- I think so and I believe I'm not mistaken-- I will be the one who will defend them, who will forget what they have done.
But since they have not informed on me, as I think-- and I think they have done me something great by not informing me.
That is also against any common sense, because why should one inform on someone?
Did the other friends knew that you were Jewish?
Nobody.
Absolutely nobody.
And these kept their mouths shut because they're afraid of their life.
Yes, always, that is Vaboulis' fiancee.
I'm the fiancee, going to get married.
I had no name.
I was called Zelma.
But I actually, Vaboulis was at the party and he went to that party purposely and he stole the passport of one of those-- what will I say-- women.
They were together and her name was, um, Ana Niedra.
So Vaboulis says in case there is a house search or what, I can present a passport.
But it's a different picture and everything.
It was a different age and all that.
That's right.
In the building company, yes.
Yes.

And then, see, he got another place for the railway station for the rail, if people wanted to travel, black marketers, they needed a special permit and he got them the permits for money, for 50 deutschmark.
And they came to our house and they brought us food, which I of course gave to small Russian children who were-- who were evacuated to Latvia, were hungry, et cetera.
That's a different story.
Well anyway, we could put even some money aside for which we bought gold because we wanted, sooner or later, we wanted to find ways how to get away.
